completed_tasks.result: Shared sentence-aware planner implemented for all engines; XTTS optimized long-form stays under conservative token cap.

completed_tasks.name: Rerun Windows plus WSL TTS scenario matrix after chunking is implemented
Add Chatterbox Turbo alternate seed samples
completed_tasks.result: optimized_seed_1337 rows and WAVs generated for Windows and WSL.

completed_tasks.name: Create structured manual quality sidecar keyed by runtime, engine, profile, and scenario
Manual listening pass over regenerated chunked TTS samples
completed_tasks.result: 16 MANUAL-QUALITY.csv rows scored: Chatterbox long, LuxTTS optimized, TADA optimized long, WSL XTTS optimized long, and Windows F5 optimized long.

completed_tasks.name: Decide whether LuxTTS should become a supported experimental engine after quality scoring
completed_tasks.result: Keep LuxTTS in future implementation as a fast experimental engine, but do not use current user-sample results to make it a default. Retest with better-padded references and other speakers/accents.

remaining_tasks.name: Optional: debug or further tune Chatterbox/TADA/F5/LuxTTS quality issues during implementation
remaining_tasks.result: Not blocking move-on. Keep all engine paths available; tune defaults/caveats from quality policy.

blockers: No hard blocker remains for moving on from Spike 003.
Quality scoring is partial, so default choices must use caveats rather than treating latency winners as automatically acceptable.
LuxTTS optimized samples have current user-sample quality failures, but the engine must remain available for future retesting and implementation.
Chatterbox Turbo baseline long-form is gibberish on both runtimes; optimized long-form normal and seed-1337 are fine on both runtimes, so the issue is not primarily seed-sensitive.
F5 optimized long-form needs stretch/duration tuning; the listened sample suggests the 1.5 setting is too aggressive.
TADA WSL optimized long-form needs caution/retest; TADA Windows optimized long-form sounded acceptable with some hiccups.

decisions.decision: Keep OMEN-PC as the only valid Phase 0 execution host and restore SSH from the repo-persisted key each session.
decisions.rationale: The earlier SEV-1 came from running backend work on the wrong machine and relying on disposable SSH state.

decisions.decision: Treat F5 and XTTS as not_applicable for attention backend labeling in the current probe.
decisions.rationale: Neither probe path exposes eager, SDPA, or FlashAttention selection; their runtime differences are streaming-path differences, not attention-backend switches.

decisions.decision: Treat Qwen3-TTS as an eager-attention baseline until flash_attn imports successfully on OMEN-PC.
decisions.rationale: The fresh labeled rerun and the FA2 install verdict both confirm that flash-attn is unavailable in this runtime.

decisions.decision: Use tts_attention_matrix.json as the source of truth for optimization backend labeling in future writeback.
decisions.rationale: The old tts_ttfa.json schema could be misread as an optimized Qwen result; the dedicated matrix makes the backend status explicit per engine.

decisions.decision: Use the pmpg Windows account for WSL-backed work on OMEN-PC; rayme-ssh remains valid for native Windows backend work.
decisions.rationale: Verified on 2026-04-22: pmpg sees Ubuntu on WSL2 and can run `wsl ls /`, while rayme-ssh reports no installed WSL distributions.

decisions.decision: For remote WSL work on OMEN-PC, prefer direct `wsl -d Ubuntu -e sh -c ...` commands with fixed Linux paths and avoid UNC WSL paths from the SSH session.
decisions.rationale: Verified on 2026-04-22: `/home/pmpg/rayme-wsl-probe` was created successfully inside Ubuntu, while `\\wsl.localhost\Ubuntu\...` and `\\wsl$\Ubuntu\...` were not reliably available from the non-interactive SSH session.

decisions.decision: Use `/home/pmpg/rayme/.venv-cu121` with `CUDA_HOME=/usr/local/cuda-12.1` as the reusable WSL accelerator env on OMEN-PC.
decisions.rationale: Verified on 2026-04-22: the env imports `torch 2.5.1+cu121` with CUDA visible on the RTX 3060 and also imports `deepspeed 0.18.9` successfully.

decisions.decision: Treat the upgraded OMEN-PC WSL env as both DeepSpeed-capable and FlashAttention-capable for future Linux-side probes.
decisions.rationale: Verified on 2026-04-23 after the in-place distro upgrade to Ubuntu 22.04.5 and glibc 2.35: `/home/pmpg/rayme/.venv-cu121` imports `torch 2.5.1+cu121`, `triton 3.1.0`, `deepspeed 0.18.9`, and `flash-attn 2.8.3` successfully on the RTX 3060.

decisions.decision: Keep F5-TTS as the current v1 default candidate while retaining XTTS as compatibility fallback.
decisions.rationale: No engine meets the target budget, and the latest OMEN-PC rerun still leaves F5 with the best TTFA while XTTS remains the only native streamer.

decisions.decision: Use the OMEN-PC WSL Docker Desktop Triton/TensorRT-LLM path as the current maximum-speed F5 runtime path, with a safe assembled model repo and CPU-preferred BLS response memory for the vocoder hop.
decisions.rationale: Verified on 2026-04-23: the stock prebuilt runtime image works after staging artifacts and applying the generated model repo patch that avoids the failing CUDA IPC output path under WSL.

decisions.decision: Do not replace the native Windows F5 short-response path with the current OMEN-PC WSL Triton path for v1 latency-sensitive acknowledgments.
decisions.rationale: Measured on 2026-04-23 with the same builder reference fixture and target text `Hey, got it.`: native Windows F5 median TTFA is 521.8 ms, while WSL Triton gRPC median TTFA is 1813.4 ms.

decisions.decision: Keep LuxTTS in future implementation as a fast experimental engine, but do not choose it as a default from current user-sample quality results.
decisions.rationale: Warm-model optimized results show LuxTTS winning several latency rows, but user listening on 2026-04-23 found sample-level quality failures. The user explicitly said this could be a bad sample and asked not to leave any engine out.

decisions.decision: Treat Chatterbox Turbo baseline long-form as quality-blocked, while keeping optimized long-form under consideration.
decisions.rationale: User listening on 2026-04-23 found baseline long-form gibberish on Windows and WSL, but normal optimized and optimized_seed_1337 long-form samples were fine on both runtimes.

decisions.decision: Keep TADA 1B as WSL-only experimental until the native Windows CUDA failure is debugged.
decisions.rationale: WSL rows produced measurable samples, but the Windows path failed during warmup/generation with a CUDA error.

decisions.decision: Treat the shared chunk planner and rerun matrix as the current source of truth for long-form TTS methodology.
decisions.rationale: XTTS long-form no longer falls back to whole-render timing; non-streaming engines now have optimized chunked-playback rows with stitched playback metrics.

decisions.decision: Do not use Chatterbox Turbo baseline/raw long-form; keep optimized chunked Chatterbox Turbo available and score short/medium before default decisions.
decisions.rationale: The alternate seed did not explain the failure: both normal optimized and seed-1337 optimized long-form samples passed manual listening, while baseline long-form failed.

decisions.decision: Keep every measured TTS engine path available in future implementation.
decisions.rationale: The user explicitly requested that no engine be left out; quality evaluations should influence defaults, warnings, labels, and retesting plans rather than removing engines entirely.

decisions.decision: Close Spike 003 as pass-with-caveats and move on.
decisions.rationale: The spike has enough measured latency and manual quality signal to guide implementation without dropping engines: keep all paths, avoid bad defaults, tune F5 stretch/duration, retest LuxTTS samples, use Chatterbox optimized long-form only, and caution TADA WSL.

decisions.decision: TADA 1B did not use an explicit fixed seed in the scenario harness.
decisions.rationale: The harness calls TADA model.generate(prompt=..., text=...) without setting or passing a seed; only Chatterbox has an alternate deterministic seed profile. Windows/WSL differences may come from stochastic state, runtime differences, or WSL model.compile.

next_action: Move on from Spike 003. Resume the main roadmap/Phase 1 work; future TTS implementation should keep all engine paths and use the recorded quality evaluations for defaults, labels, warnings, and retest priorities.
context_notes: Spike 003 is closed as PASS_WITH_CAVEATS. Shared TTS chunk planner is implemented and the full Windows plus WSL matrix reran on OMEN-PC. Sixteen manual quality rows are recorded. Keep every measured engine path in future implementation. LuxTTS is fastest but poor for the current sample; Chatterbox baseline long is gibberish but optimized long is fine; TADA Windows long is acceptable while WSL long is caution; XTTS WSL long is metallic/hiccupy and sample-sensitive; F5 Windows long likely suffers from an over-aggressive 1.5 stretch/duration setting.

incident.summary: Phase 0 execution previously ran on the wrong machine and lost SSH persistence, forcing repeated recovery loops.
incident.user_impact: Multiple days of avoidable churn and justified loss of trust.
prevention_rules: Use OMEN-PC only for Phase 0 backend work.
Restore SSH from .local/phase0-ssh/ each fresh session.
Verify hostname, whoami, python --version, and nvidia-smi before claiming backend access works.
Prefer the shortest validated recovery path.
Never run rm -rf on project or backend hosts.
Never delete files or directories through variable-expanded paths.

manual_quality_findings: User reported Chatterbox Turbo baseline long-form is gibberish on Windows and WSL.
User reported Chatterbox Turbo optimized long-form is fine on Windows and WSL.
User reported Chatterbox Turbo optimized_seed_1337 long-form is fine on Windows and WSL.

User reported LuxTTS optimized short/medium/long on Windows and WSL has quality failures: sample-tail repetition, metallic noise, too-fast delivery, sample phrase/word leakage, poor speaker match, and accent loss.
User reported TADA 1B Windows optimized long sounds acceptable with some hiccups.
User reported TADA 1B WSL optimized long sounds worse than Windows, less steady, with voice morphing and hiccups.
User reported WSL XTTS optimized long sounds metallic and hiccupy, with incomplete voice likeness; XTTS is known from prior user testing to be sample-sensitive.
User reported Windows F5 optimized long sounds stretched/slow and hiccupy, degrading into mumbling/silence before recovery; the 1.5 multiplier is likely too much.
User explicitly asked to keep every engine in future implementation; treat quality results as defaults/caveats/retest inputs, not removal decisions.
streaming_caveats: XTTS optimized rows use native streaming where available; optimized long-form is measured over planner chunks and no longer falls back because of the 400-token cap.
F5, LuxTTS, Chatterbox Turbo, TADA 1B, and Qwen optimized rows use shared chunked playback, not true incremental decoder streaming.
Qwen3 rows are simulated text/chunk playback and remain far too slow for product default use.
Quality findings must override raw latency for defaults and user-facing recommendations.
required_harness_fixes: Teach future TTS decision logic to read MANUAL-QUALITY.csv or an equivalent quality policy so quality findings override raw latency wins for defaults while preserving all engine paths.
Tune or disable the F5 long-form 1.5 stretch/duration multiplier and rerun/listen before relying on that optimized path.
Retest LuxTTS with better-padded reference audio and other speakers/accents before treating current sample failures as engine-wide behavior.
If TADA determinism matters, add explicit seed support around the TADA generation path and compare Windows vs WSL again.
tts_scenario_matrix_v2.next_run: No immediate rerun required to move on. Future targeted reruns: F5 with lower/no stretch multiplier, LuxTTS with better-padded references, and TADA with explicit seeding if reproducibility matters.
tts_scenario_matrix_v2.pause_note: Closed pass-with-caveats. Keep all TTS engines in future implementation; quality findings shape defaults and caveats.
